name: Skanda Purana
shortDescription: The Skanda Purana, the largest of the eighteen Puranas, is dedicated primarily to the deity Skanda (Kartikeya), narrating his birth, deeds, and significance.
description: The Skanda Purana, one of the eighteen major Puranas in Hinduism, stands as the largest Purana, comprising over 81,000 verses. While named after and primarily dedicated to Skanda, also known as Kartikeya, the god of war and son of Shiva and Parvati, the text is a diverse compilation covering a wide range of subjects. It encompasses mythology, cosmology, pilgrimage sites (Tirthas), religious practices, and moral teachings. The Skanda Purana is not a unified text, but rather a collection of sections (khandas) and sub-sections (mahatmyas) that were likely composed and appended over centuries by various authors. Its significance lies in its detailed descriptions of pilgrimage sites across India, making it a valuable resource for understanding the religious geography and practices of the Hindu tradition. It provides insights into the development of various cults and devotional practices, particularly those associated with Shiva, Vishnu, and Shakti, in addition to Skanda himself. The text underscores the importance of dharma, karma, and bhakti as paths to spiritual liberation.
subtitle: A tapestry of tales, pilgrimage, and devotion.
author: Traditionally attributed to Vyasa
significance: Provides a comprehensive overview of Hindu religious practices, pilgrimage sites, and mythology, especially concerning Skanda/Kartikeya.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Skanda Purana's origins are complex, as it's not the work of a single author or period. It likely began taking shape around the 8th century CE, with additions and revisions continuing until the 15th century CE. The core narrative revolves around Skanda's birth and his heroic deeds, particularly his victory over the demon Taraka. The mythology within the Skanda Purana also encompasses a vast array of stories related to Shiva, Parvati, Vishnu, and other deities. It includes detailed accounts of various battles between gods and demons, as well as the origins of sacred sites and rivers. The text often emphasizes the importance of Shiva and Shakti worship, weaving together various strands of Shaiva, Vaishnava, and Shakta traditions. The stories are often framed within the context of dialogues between sages and disciples, providing a framework for conveying religious and philosophical teachings.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Skanda Purana plays a significant role in Hindu religious life, particularly for those who follow Skanda/Kartikeya. It serves as a guide for pilgrimage, detailing the locations, histories, and rituals associated with numerous sacred sites throughout India. Its descriptions of these Tirthas (pilgrimage sites) make it an invaluable resource for understanding the religious geography of the country. The text also provides guidance on religious practices, including fasting, festivals, and the worship of various deities. The Skanda Purana's emphasis on dharma, karma, and bhakti reinforces core Hindu values and provides a framework for ethical conduct. It also serves as a repository of cultural and historical information, offering insights into the social and religious life of ancient India. The stories and teachings within the Skanda Purana continue to inspire devotion and inform religious practices among Hindus today.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Skanda Purana, like other Puranas, is rich in symbolism. Skanda himself symbolizes valor, leadership, and the triumph of good over evil. His six heads represent his multifaceted nature and his ability to perceive reality from multiple perspectives. The stories of the gods and demons often represent the internal struggles between the forces of good and evil within the human psyche. The pilgrimage sites described in the Skanda Purana can be interpreted as symbolic representations of the journey towards spiritual liberation. The rivers and mountains associated with these sites symbolize purification and transcendence. The rituals and practices prescribed in the text can be seen as symbolic acts that help to purify the mind and body, leading to a deeper understanding of the self and the divine. The text encourages readers to interpret the stories and teachings on multiple levels, both literally and symbolically, to gain a deeper understanding of their meaning.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Skanda Purana's influence extends across various aspects of Hindu culture and religious practice. Its descriptions of pilgrimage sites continue to guide devotees to sacred locations throughout India. The stories and teachings within the text have been adapted and retold in various forms, including dramas, dances, and visual arts. The Skanda Purana's emphasis on Skanda/Kartikeya has contributed to the widespread worship of this deity, particularly in South India, where he is known as Murugan. The text has also influenced the development of various Hindu sects and devotional movements. Its teachings on dharma, karma, and bhakti continue to shape the ethical and spiritual values of Hindus today. The Skanda Purana remains a valuable resource for scholars and researchers interested in understanding the history, mythology, and religious practices of Hinduism.

sources.author: G.V. Tagare
sources.description: A comprehensive English translation of the Skanda Purana, providing access to the text for non-Sanskrit readers.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.description: Provides context and analysis of the Puranas, including the Skanda Purana, within the broader framework of Hindu history and mythology.

associations.name: Spear (Vel)
associations.significance: Associated with Skanda, symbolizing power, protection, and the destruction of ignorance.

associations.name: Peacock
associations.significance: Skanda's vahana (mount), representing beauty, victory, and the subjugation of negative forces.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Ares/Mars
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are gods of war, embodying courage and martial prowess.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Ogmios
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A god of eloquence and persuasion, sometimes depicted as a warrior figure, paralleling Skanda's leadership qualities.

companions.name: Shiva
companions.description: Skanda's father, the supreme god in Shaivism, representing destruction and regeneration.

companions.name: Parvati
companions.description: Skanda's mother, the divine consort of Shiva, representing power, love, and motherhood.

companions.name: Ganesha
companions.description: Skanda's brother, the god of wisdom and remover of obstacles, revered for his intelligence and auspiciousness.

worshipPractices.name: Kavadi Attam
worshipPractices.description: A ceremonial act of offering during the worship of Murugan (Skanda), involving carrying a decorated kavadi (burden) to the temple.

worshipPractices.name: Vel Pooja
worshipPractices.description: Worship of the Vel (spear), Skanda's divine weapon, symbolizing the destruction of evil.

festivals.name: Skanda Shashti
festivals.timing: Sixth day of Shukla Paksha in the month of Kartika (October-November)
festivals.description: A six-day festival dedicated to Skanda, celebrating his victory over the demon Taraka.

festivals.name: Thaipusam
festivals.timing: Full moon day in the Tamil month of Thai (January-February)
festivals.description: A major festival dedicated to Murugan (Skanda), celebrated with processions, kavadi offerings, and devotional practices.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Skanda Purana continues to inspire religious practices and artistic expressions related to Skanda/Kartikeya, particularly in South India and among the Tamil diaspora. Its stories and teachings are adapted and retold in various media, contributing to the ongoing vitality of Hindu traditions.
